text: The surgeon divides the round ligament of the liver, followed by the suspensory ligament, progressing towards the line of the hepatic veins. The left triangular ligament is then divided, and the right triangular ligament is released, revealing the edge of the right hepatic vein.

The surgeon raises the lower face of the liver and opens the lesser omentum to reach the aranchium ligament. Blunt maneuvers are used to detach the Lien X capsule, following Takasaki and Sujika's concepts, and Gate III is identified.
The surgeon isolates the left hilar pedicle and temporarily clamps it to define the ischemic zone for an anatomic hepatectomy. Indocyanine green is applied to visually demarcate the line of ischemia.
The surgical robot displays a coupled image of the intraoperative ultrasound, showing the middle and left hepatic veins. The tumor is located near the outlet of the middle hepatic vein.

The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma using robotic instruments that combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing. Maryland bipolar forceps are employed for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. As the partition progresses, polymer clips are applied as needed. The veins in segments IVB and IVA are identified, followed by thorough cleaning for sealing and sectioning.

The surgeon uses a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line to section the left hilar pedicle, adjusting the staple size to match the tissue thickness. Three-point-five-millimeter staples are used for the pedicle, while two-point-five-millimeter staples are applied to the left hepatic vein. The procedure is performed with a short Pringle maneuver in place.

The surgical specimen is placed into a plastic retrieval bag. The surgeon checks the hemostasis of the liver parenchyma and the staple line sections. Indocyanine green is used to verify the absence of ischemic areas.
